𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Thirty-Four -- Complete

The Visions of Amram

4QAmram

An Aramaic Vision of the Two Spirits -- DSS Attested

Columns 1 through 3

Two Figures Were Contending Over Me -- Melkiresha Ruler of Darkness -- Melchizedek Prince of Light -- I Have Been Given to the Portion of the Sons of Light -- Walk in the Paths of Light All Your Days

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

Dead Sea Scrolls -- Cave 4 Qumran -- 4Q543-548 -- Aramaic Primary

Attributed to Amram Father of Mosheh Aharon and Miriam

Earliest Formulation of the Two Spirits Doctrine -- Predates the Community Rule

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

VISIONS OF AMRAM -- THE TWO SPIRITS -- COLUMNS 1 THROUGH 3

VISIONS OF AMRAM — COLUMN 1 Amram Son of Kehat — He Lay Down and Saw a Vision — Two Figures Were Contending Over Him — One Was Bright and One Was Dark — Their Dominion Extended Over All Flesh

1 A copy of the book of the words of the vision of Amram son of Kehat son of Levi, all that he revealed to his sons and charged them with on the day he died, in the one hundred and thirty-seventh year of his age, in the year of his death.

2 He called Mosheh his son and Aharon and Miriam his daughter and told them all that he had seen and all that he had been charged with.

3 I lay down upon my bed and I saw a vision of darkness, and behold two figures were contending over me and striving for me.

4 And I asked them: Who are you, that you have authority over me?

5 And they said to me: We have been given authority over all flesh. We rule over all the sons of man.

6 And I asked the one whose appearance was frightening — all his works were darkness — who are you?

7 And he answered me and said: I am the Watcher Melkiresha, ruler of all darkness, and my dominion is over all evil.

8 And I looked and I saw the other whose countenance was like the sun and whose raiment was shining light.

9 And I asked him: And you — who are you?

10 And he said to me: I am Melchizedek, the great Prince of Light, and I have authority over all the sons of light.

11 And the two of them contended and strove over me until morning.

12 And I did not know which of them would prevail over me and to whose portion I would belong.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Visions of Amram (4QAmram) was found in Cave 4 at Qumran in multiple Aramaic fragments designated 4Q543-548. The text is attributed to Amram, father of Mosheh, Aharon, and Miriam. It presents a deathbed vision in which two angelic figures contend for authority over Amram — one representing darkness and one representing light. This is the earliest surviving explicit formulation of the Two Spirits doctrine that the Community Rule develops in Columns 3-4. The Visions of Amram predates the Community Rule and establishes the theological foundation of the entire Qumran dualistic framework.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez tradition does not preserve the Visions of Amram as a separate text. However the covenant framework of two contending powers — one of light and one of darkness — operating over human lives is consistent with the broader Orit Ge'ez cosmological tradition preserved in Chanokh. The naming of the two figures in Column 1 — Melkiresha as ruler of darkness and Melchizedek as Prince of Light — grounds the cosmic dualism in specific named beings whose authority over human destiny is absolute until the final judgment.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic tradition does not preserve the Visions of Amram. Amram appears in the Masoretic canon only as the father of Mosheh and Aharon in Shemot 6:18-20 and in the genealogies of Bamidbar and Divrei HaYamim. The Qumran tradition substantially expands Amram's role: he is a patriarch who receives a major apocalyptic vision and transmits covenant knowledge to his children — including Mosheh — before his death. The vision attributes the Two Spirits framework to the generation immediately before the Exodus, grounding it in the earliest period of covenant formation.

[CRITICAL NOTE] The identification of the Prince of Light as Melchizedek in verse 10 directly connects the Visions of Amram to the 11QMelchizedek text from Cave 11 — where Melchizedek is the heavenly figure who executes final judgment on Belial. Both texts are part of the same Qumran theological tradition in which Melchizedek is not merely the ancient king of Shalem from Bereshit 14 but an ongoing heavenly figure whose authority over the sons of light is active throughout covenant history. The Visions of Amram establishes this theology at the earliest possible point — before the Exodus itself.

VISIONS OF AMRAM — COLUMN 2 The Ruler of Darkness Describes His Kingdom — The Prince of Light Describes His — Amram Is Given to the Portion of Light — All the Sons of Light Shall Walk in Eternal Light — The Sons of Darkness in Eternal Darkness

1 And Melkiresha said to me: My dominion is over all sons of wickedness and all their works are darkness.

2 I lead them in the paths of darkness and I teach them works of destruction and violence.

3 And all who belong to my portion shall go down into the pit of fire and into the darkness of the deep.

4 And Melchizedek said to me: All who belong to my portion shall walk in eternal light and shall inherit all the good things of heaven.

5 And I will set them apart and deliver them from the hands of all the spirits of wickedness.

6 And all the sons of light shall have peace and blessing and shall live forever.

7 And the ruler of darkness said: Do not listen to him, for you are in my hand and your portion is with me.

8 But I said to the ruler of darkness: You have no authority over me, for I have been given to the portion of the sons of light.

9 And the ruler of darkness departed from me and I saw the Prince of Light standing before me in great glory.

10 And he said to me: Be strong, Amram, and fear not, for you are mine and your portion is with the sons of light forever.

11 And I awoke from my vision and I was filled with great joy, for I knew that I belonged to the portion of light.

12 And I called my children Mosheh and Aharon and Miriam and told them all these things that they should know which portion they belong to and in which paths they should walk.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4Q544 and 4Q545 preserve substantial portions of Column 2 in Aramaic. The resolution of the vision — Amram is given to the portion of light — is the theological climax of the text. The two-portion framework — sons of light and sons of darkness — is the same framework the Community Rule's Two Spirits exposition employs, but here it is presented in the vivid personal form of a patriarch choosing his portion in a direct encounter with the two ruling figures. The transmission of this vision to Mosheh, Aharon, and Miriam places the Two Spirits doctrine at the foundational moment of covenant formation.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez tradition does not preserve the Visions of Amram. The covenant framework of the vision — Creator's people belonging to the portion of light while the wicked belong to the portion of darkness — resonates throughout the Orit Ge'ez wisdom tradition, particularly in the Chanokh Epistle's contrast between the generation of light and those born in darkness. The Visions of Amram grounds this cosmic distinction in a specific covenantal moment: Amram's encounter with the two powers establishes the framework that Mosheh will carry into the Exodus and covenant formation at Sinai.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic tradition does not preserve the Visions of Amram. The covenant theology of light and darkness — the two portions — has its Masoretic resonances in Devarim 30:15-20 — I have set before you life and death, blessing and curse, therefore choose life. The Visions of Amram presents this choice not as an abstract moral decision but as a cosmic encounter in which a patriarch literally faces the two ruling powers and asserts his covenant identity. The choice of life in the Mosaic covenant has its personal antecedent in Amram's vision.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 8 — I said to the ruler of darkness: You have no authority over me, for I have been given to the portion of the sons of light. Amram's declaration of covenant identity to the ruler of darkness is the personal covenant affirmation that mirrors the Community Rule's annual covenant renewal ceremony of Column 2 — where members confess their sins and reaffirm their belonging to the covenant. The Visions of Amram establishes that this covenant identity is not self-generated but divinely given: Amram does not choose the light through his own virtue — he has been given to the portion of the sons of light by the Prince of Light himself.

VISIONS OF AMRAM — COLUMN 3 Amram Charges His Sons — Walk in the Paths of Light — Do Not Walk in the Ways of Darkness — The Inheritance of the Sons of Light — The Eternal Covenant Shall Stand

1 And now my sons hear me and walk in the paths of light and do not walk in the paths of darkness.

2 For the paths of light lead to eternal life and the paths of darkness lead to eternal death.

3 And you Mosheh my son — you shall be the servant of YHWH and shall lead the people of the covenant out of the land of bondage.

4 And you Aharon — you shall be the high priest of YHWH and shall minister before him in the sanctuary that he will establish.

5 And you Miriam — you shall prophesy before the people and shall lead the daughters of Yisra'EL in praise before YHWH.

6 And all the sons of light shall be with you and the Prince of Light shall be your strength.

7 Walk therefore in truth and in righteousness all your days and teach your children to do the same.

8 And do not mix with the sons of darkness or take from their table or share in their portion.

9 For their end is the pit and their inheritance is destruction and their days are numbered.

10 But your inheritance is eternal life and your days shall not be cut off.

11 And the covenant which YHWH made with your fathers Avraham and Yitzhak and Ya'akov shall stand forever.

12 And I Amram have written down all these things that they may be kept for all the generations of the sons of light forever and ever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4Q546 and 4Q547 preserve portions of Column 3 in Aramaic. The deathbed charges of Amram to his three children — Mosheh, Aharon, and Miriam — each assigned their specific covenant role — are the practical application of the vision. The assignment of roles confirms the Qumran community's priestly self-understanding: Aharon the priest stands first in the covenant hierarchy, followed by Mosheh the lawgiver and Miriam the prophet. The explicit naming of all three reflects the Qumran community's recognition of Miriam's prophetic role — a recognition the Masoretic tradition preserves in Shemot 15:20 but does not develop.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves all three figures — Mosheh, Aharon, and Miriam — in the Shemot narrative. The Visions of Amram establishes their covenant roles before their births through their father's dying vision. The covenant community's understanding of its own structure — priest, teacher, and prophet — is here rooted in the Amram tradition: the covenant people are constituted by these three offices, each with its specific function in the transmission of the covenant.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Bamidbar 12 preserves the tradition that Miriam was a prophet alongside Mosheh and Aharon — YHWH said: hear now my words: if there is a prophet among you, I YHWH make myself known to him in a vision — and Miriam's role as prophet is affirmed. The Visions of Amram grounds this recognition in Amram's dying vision: Miriam is not a secondary figure granted prophetic status late — she is designated a prophet by her father's covenant vision before the Exodus.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12 — I Amram have written down all these things that they may be kept for all the generations of the sons of light forever and ever. The Book of Giants ends with Chanokh writing the decree on heavenly tablets; the Visions of Amram ends with Amram writing down his vision for all the generations of the sons of light. The writing covenant — the obligation to preserve and transmit covenant revelation in written form — is the consistent thread from Chanokh through the patriarchal traditions to the Qumran community itself, which understood its own scribal work as the direct continuation of this chain.

VISIONS OF AMRAM -- COMPLETE

Volume Thirty-Four -- The Ancient Covenant Record

𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

I HAVE BEEN GIVEN TO THE PORTION OF THE SONS OF LIGHT

AND THE PRINCE OF LIGHT SHALL BE YOUR STRENGTH

Visions of Amram Column 2 -- Dead Sea Scrolls 4Q543-548
